EnteroScope Ultimate Analysis Report

Gene-Centric Cross-Genome Analysis for Enterobacter cloacae Complex

2026-05-11 12:54:07
Samples: 100
Pathogen: Enterobacter cloacae complex
University of Ghana Medical School
🧬 Enterobacter cloacae produces a variety of siderophores (enterobactin, aerobactin) for iron acquisition, enhancing its virulence in iron-limited host environments.

📊 Executive Summary – Enterobacter cloacae Complex Analysis

What this tab shows: A high-level overview of all analyzed genomes, including total sample count, MLST diversity, AMR/virulence gene frequencies, and detection of critical resistance (carbapenemases, colistin, tigecycline).

Why it matters for E. cloacae research: Enterobacter cloacae complex is an emerging multidrug-resistant pathogen associated with hospital outbreaks. Understanding population structure and resistance gene frequencies is essential for surveillance, infection control, and treatment guidance.

How to interpret: High numbers of unique STs suggest genetic diversity, while dominant STs indicate potential outbreak clones. Elevated carbapenemase counts (alert box) signal a high-risk CRE scenario requiring immediate intervention.

⚠️ Critical Resistance Alert

33 carbapenemase genes detected across samples. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) are a critical public health threat with mortality rates >40%. Immediate infection control measures recommended.

⚠️ Environmental Co-Selection Alert

315 environmental resistance markers detected (biocides/heavy metals). These genes can co-select for antibiotic resistance in hospital environments, promoting persistence despite disinfection.

Key Statistics

MetricCountDetails
Total Samples Analyzed100Complete genomic analysis
Sequence Types (MLST)45Enterobacter MLST scheme
Total AMR Genes756Across all AMR databases
Carbapenemase Genes33KPC, NDM, VIM, IMP, OXA-48 types
Virulence Genes207Biofilm, iron uptake, toxins
Environmental Markers315Heavy metal, biocide, stress response
High-Risk Isolates40Carbapenemase + last-resort resistance

🧪 Sample Overview – Per-Genome Metadata

What this tab shows: Each genome's MLST sequence type (ST), virulence gene count, and assembly quality metrics (N50, GC%, AT%).

Why it matters: Linking resistance/virulence genotypes to specific isolates enables outbreak tracking, clonal comparison, and identification of hypervirulent or high-risk clones. MLST provides a portable nomenclature for global epidemiology.

How to interpret: High virulence counts (e.g., >10) suggest enhanced pathogenic potential. Low N50 indicates fragmented assemblies that may miss genes. ST clustering indicates related isolates – consider if from same patient/ward/time period.

🔬 FASTA Quality Control – Assembly Metrics

What this tab shows: Key assembly statistics per genome: N50 (contig length at 50% of assembly), GC%, AT%, number of contigs, total length, etc.

Why it matters: Assembly quality directly affects gene detection accuracy. Poor assemblies (high contig count, low N50) may miss resistance/virulence genes or produce false positives. GC% deviation from ~55% (typical for E. cloacae) might indicate contamination.

How to interpret: N50 > 50 kb indicates good assembly; N50 > 100 kb is excellent. Total length 4.5–5.5 Mbp is expected. GC% 54–56% is normal. High contig count (>500) suggests fragmented assembly – consider re-assembly or long-read sequencing.

🧬 MLST – Sequence Typing and Population Structure

What this tab shows: Distribution of sequence types (STs) among isolates, based on the Enterobacter cloacae MLST scheme (7 housekeeping genes: fusA, rpoB, etc.).

Why it matters: MLST reveals clonal relationships, outbreak clusters, and internationally disseminated high-risk clones (e.g., ST171, ST78, ST114, ST418). Certain STs are associated with carbapenemase acquisition and hospital spread.

How to interpret: A single dominant ST suggests a common source outbreak. Multiple unique STs indicate sporadic cases or environmental diversity. Novel STs (not in PubMLST) may represent new lineages. Cross-reference STs with AMR profiles to identify high-risk clones.

Detected 45 unique STs among 89 isolates.

💊 Antimicrobial Resistance Genes – Comprehensive AMR Profile

What this tab shows: All detected AMR genes across multiple databases (AMRfinder, CARD, ResFinder, etc.), including carbapenemases, ESBLs, colistin resistance (mcr), and tigecycline resistance (tet(X), efflux pumps).

Why it matters for E. cloacae: This species is a major CRE pathogen. Detecting carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA-48) guides therapy (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam vs. aztreonam-avibactam). Colistin resistance (mcr) or tigecycline resistance predicts treatment failure of last-resort drugs.

How to interpret: Sort by frequency to see common resistance determinants. Click on filter buttons to focus on high-priority genes. The "Genomes" column lists which isolates carry the gene – use the second search box to highlight specific genomes across the table.

GeneDatabaseFrequencyGenomes

🦠 Virulence Factors – Pathogenicity Determinants

What this tab shows: Virulence-associated genes including biofilm formation (ompA, pgaABCD, csg), adhesion, type VI secretion systems (T6SS), iron acquisition (enterobactin, aerobactin), and toxins.

Why it matters: Biofilm genes enable persistent infections on medical devices (catheters, ventilators). Iron acquisition systems enhance survival in host tissues. T6SS mediates interbacterial competition and may modulate host immune response.

How to interpret: High prevalence of biofilm genes suggests these isolates are capable of forming recalcitrant biofilms – important for infection control. Search/click filters to check specific virulence mechanisms. Cross-reference with AMR genes to identify isolates with both high resistance and high virulence potential.

GeneDatabaseFrequencyGenomes

🧪 Biocide & Heavy Metal Resistance – Environmental Co-Selection Markers

What this tab shows: Genes conferring resistance to disinfectants (qac, cepA, form), heavy metals (mercury – mer, arsenic – ars, copper – cop, silver – sil), and other environmental stressors.

Why it matters: These markers often co-locate with AMR genes on mobile genetic elements (plasmids, integrons). Their presence indicates potential co-selection of antibiotic resistance in hospital environments exposed to biocides and heavy metals (e.g., from disinfectants, plumbing, medical devices).

How to interpret: High prevalence of qac genes suggests reduced susceptibility to quaternary ammonium compounds (common disinfectants). Mercury/arsenic resistance may indicate historical or ongoing heavy metal exposure. Search for specific markers to assess disinfection efficacy risk.

GeneDatabaseFrequencyGenomes

🧬 Plasmid Replicons – Mobile Genetic Elements

What this tab shows: Plasmid replicon markers (e.g., IncL/M, IncFII, IncFIB, IncHI2, Col-like) and their distribution among isolates.

Why it matters for E. cloacae: Epidemic carbapenemase genes (blaOXA-48, blaKPC, blaNDM) are often carried on specific plasmids: IncL/M frequently carries blaOXA-48, IncFII/IncFIB often carry blaKPC/blaCTX-M, and IncHI2 may carry mcr or NDM. Identifying plasmid replicons helps predict resistance gene mobility and outbreak potential.

How to interpret: High-frequency replicons indicate successful plasmid backbones spreading in the population. Presence of IncL/M + blaOXA-48 suggests a transferable carbapenem resistance threat. Multiple replicons in the same isolate may indicate plasmid accumulation. If no data, run PlasmidFinder separately.

⚠️ High‑Risk Isolates – Carbapenemase + Last‑Line Resistance

What this tab shows: Isolates that co‑carry carbapenemase genes AND resistance to at least one last‑resort antibiotic (colistin, tigecycline).

Why it matters: These "pan-resistant" isolates leave virtually no treatment options. Their emergence signals a critical threat requiring urgent public health response, infection control, and alternative therapies (e.g., cefiderocol, phage therapy).

How to interpret: Each row lists the sample, ST, and specific carbapenemase/colistin/tigecycline genes. Cross-reference with MLST tab to see if high‑risk isolates share the same ST (possible outbreak). Immediate action: isolate patients, enhanced environmental cleaning, and contact precautions.

🔗 Gene Co‑occurrence (Top 500 Pairs)

What this shows: Pairs of genes that frequently appear together in the same genome. High co‑occurrence may indicate physical linkage (same plasmid, integron, or genomic island) or shared selection pressure.

How to use: Use the search box below to filter by gene name. Sort by count to find the most common associations (e.g., carbapenemase + efflux pump, or mcr + Inc plasmid).

📚 Database Metrics – Gene Coverage Summary

What this tab shows: Per-database statistics: number of unique genes, total occurrences (sum of gene counts across genomes), and critical genes (carbapenemases, ESBLs, colistin, tigecycline).

Why it matters: Different databases have varying coverage and specificity. AMRfinder provides FDA-ARGOS curated genes, CARD includes resistance ontology, ResFinder focuses on acquired resistance, and BacMet covers biocides/metals.

How to interpret: Larger unique gene counts indicate broader coverage. High total occurrences suggest widespread resistance determinants. Compare databases to identify genes found by multiple tools (higher confidence) vs. unique hits (may need validation).

🤖 AI Assistant Integration Guide

What this tab shows: Instructions for using large language models (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek) to analyze your Enterobacter cloacae genome data.

Why it matters: AI can help interpret complex resistance patterns, suggest treatment correlations, generate hypotheses, and draft reports – accelerating research and clinical decision-making.

How to use: Upload this HTML file or the companion JSON file to your preferred AI assistant. Then ask questions such as:

  • Which samples carry carbapenemase genes?
  • List all isolates with high biofilm‑related gene counts.
  • What are the most common biocide resistance genes in this dataset?
  • Show me ST-plasmid associations.
  • Which high-risk isolates require immediate infection control?
  • Summarize the AMR profile of sample X.

The AI can also generate custom tables, maps, and even draft manuscripts based on these data.

🌍 Call to Action – Combating AMR Together

Global burden: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) kills an estimated 1.27 million people annually, with carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) classified as a critical priority pathogen by WHO. Enterobacter cloacae complex is a major contributor to hospital-acquired CRE infections worldwide.

Concrete actions you can take:

  • Share data to public databases: Submit genomes and resistance profiles to NCBI (BioProject), PubMLST, and PLSDB to enable global surveillance.
  • Implement local surveillance: Establish routine genomic surveillance of Enterobacter cloacae isolates in your hospital or region to detect emerging resistance early.
  • Adopt infection control measures: Use contact precautions, enhanced environmental disinfection, and antimicrobial stewardship to limit spread of CRE.
  • Contribute to open science: Improve EnteroScope, report bugs, suggest features, or fork the repository on GitHub. Your contributions benefit the entire research community.
  • Collaborate with us: Reach out for collaborations, data sharing, or joint grant applications. We welcome partnerships with clinicians, epidemiologists, and bioinformaticians.

⭐ Star us on GitHub ⭐

📥 Export Data – CSV and JSON Formats

What this tab shows: Buttons to download tables (CSV) for further analysis in Excel, R, or Python, and the full JSON report for programmatic access or AI upload.

Why it matters: Enables custom plots, statistical analyses, metadata integration, and sharing with collaborators who prefer tabular data.

How to use: Click any CSV button to download the current filtered/sorted table. The JSON file contains the complete integrated dataset (use the link after report generation).
